turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: In case number 17-5554, Stokeling v. United States Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on a Writ of Certiorari to United States Court of Appeals for the Eleventh Circuit. Petitioner Stokeling pleaded guilty to possessing a firearm after having been convicted of a felony in violation of 18 U.S.C 922(g)(1). He had previously been convicted of home invasion, kidnapping and robbery. The probation office recommended that Stokeling be sentenced under the Arm Career Criminal Act, commonly known as ACCA. That statute provides that a person convicted under 922(g) who has three previous convictions for violent felony shall be imprisoned for minimum of 15 years. As relevant here, ACCA defines a violent felony to include any crime punishable by imprisonment for a term exceeding one year that has as an element the use, attempted use or threaten to use of physical force against a person of another. Stokeling objected that his prior conviction for Florida robbery did not satisfy this provision, referred to as the Elements Clause. The Florida Supreme Court has explained that the use of force necessary to commit robbery is resistance to the victim that is overcome by the physical force of the offender. The district court concluded that the ACCA enhancement did not apply and the Eleventh Circuit reversed. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we affirm. Florida's requirement that the defendant overcome the victim's resistance qualifies as physical force under the Elements Clause. We construe the language of the Elements Clause in light of the statutory history of ACCA and our 2010 opinion in Johnson v. United States. As originally enacted in 1984, ACCA specifically included robbery as a qualifying offense and defined it to require force or violence, which mirrored the elements of the common-law crime of robbery. At common law, the term force and violence were used interchangeably in the robbery context to mean that a victim's resistance must be overcome, however slight the resistance might be. Two years later, Congress amended ACCA. The amendment expanded the predicate offenses by replacing robbery and burglary with the current Elements Clause, a new list of enumerated offenses and a now-defunct residual clause. The new Elements Clause has the same touchstone as the original robbery definition force. By retaining the term 'force' in the current version of ACCA and otherwise expanding the predicate offenses, Congress made clear that the force required for common-law robbery would be sufficient to satisfy the new Elements Clause. Our understanding of physical force is supported by the state's definition of robbery in 1986 a majority of which would not qualify as ACCA predicates under Stokeling's reading. Our interpretation also comports with the Court's opinion in Johnson which held that the level of force necessary to commit misdemeanor battery did not satisfy ACCA. Johnson explained that the slightest offensive touching was insufficient because the Elements Clause requires force capable of causing physical pain or injury to another person. Florida robbery satisfies that definition because it requires force that overpowers a victim's resistance, which necessarily involves a physical struggle. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we affirm the judgment of the Eleventh Circuit. Justice Sotomayor has filed a dissenting opinion in which the Chief Justice, Justice Ginsburg and Justice Kagan have joined.
